specification.topic-statement: The scroll detector classifies arrow-key events as either mouse-wheel scroll events or keyboard arrow-key events using a timed state machine.

scope.in-scope: Detector creation, arrow-event classification, timeout computation, timeout-triggered flushing, and immediate flushing.
scope.boundary: The caller delivers arrow events (up or down) paired with millisecond timestamps. The detector never reads the clock itself. The source of timestamps — system clock, event loop, or test harness — is outside scope.
The caller receives a classification result for each operation and decides what to do with it. How scroll and arrow events are acted upon is outside scope.

field.name: result
field.note: one of: none, absorbed, scroll-up, scroll-down, arrow-up, arrow-down

field.name: milliseconds
field.note: -1 means no timeout needed; 0 means already expired; positive means milliseconds until flush

behavior.detector-creation:
A new detector is created. Its state is set to idle. The burst threshold is set to 5 milliseconds. The internal timer is initialized to zero. The pending direction is set to up as an arbitrary placeholder; this value is meaningless while the detector is idle.
If the parent context is null, the process aborts before any allocation is attempted. If memory allocation fails, the process aborts immediately.
The struct field storing the burst threshold carries the comment "Timer duration (20ms)," but the value assigned during creation is 5 milliseconds.
behavior.arrow-processing:
An arrow direction and a millisecond timestamp are submitted. The detector computes the elapsed time as the difference between the provided timestamp and the timestamp recorded when the current timer was last started.
The detector's response depends on its current state and the elapsed time.
When idle, the detector records the arrow direction as the pending direction, records the current timestamp as the timer start, transitions to the waiting state, and returns none. The arrow is buffered; no classification is emitted.
When waiting and the elapsed time is less than or equal to 5 milliseconds, a mouse-wheel burst is detected. The detector emits a scroll-up result if the pending direction (the first arrow's direction) is up, or a scroll-down result if the pending direction is down. The timer start is reset to the current timestamp. The detector transitions to the absorbing state.
The scroll direction is determined by the pending direction, which is the direction of the first arrow in the burst. The direction of the second arrow that triggered burst detection is not used and has no effect on the emitted result.
When waiting and the elapsed time exceeds 5 milliseconds, the pending arrow is treated as a keyboard arrow. The detector emits an arrow-up result if the pending direction is up, or arrow-down if the pending direction is down. The arriving arrow then replaces the pending direction, and the timer start is reset to the current timestamp. The detector remains in the waiting state.
The old pending event is flushed and the new arrow is immediately buffered in a single operation. The state never transitions through idle; it stays in waiting for the newly buffered arrow.
When absorbing and the elapsed time is less than or equal to 5 milliseconds, the arriving arrow is part of the ongoing burst. The timer start is reset to the current timestamp. The detector returns absorbed and remains in the absorbing state.
When absorbing and the elapsed time exceeds 5 milliseconds, the burst has ended. The arriving arrow is recorded as the new pending direction. The timer start is reset to the current timestamp. The detector transitions to the waiting state and returns none.
behavior.timeout-checking:
A millisecond timestamp is submitted. The detector checks whether a buffered event has been waiting longer than the burst threshold.
When idle, none is returned immediately. No elapsed time is computed.
When waiting or absorbing, and the elapsed time is less than or equal to 5 milliseconds, none is returned. The timer has not expired.
When waiting and the elapsed time exceeds 5 milliseconds, the single buffered arrow is classified as a keyboard event. The detector emits arrow-up if the pending direction is up, or arrow-down if the pending direction is down. The detector transitions to idle.
When absorbing and the elapsed time exceeds 5 milliseconds, the burst window has closed. The detector transitions to idle and returns none. No event is emitted because the scroll event was already emitted when the burst was first detected.
behavior.timeout-computation:
A millisecond timestamp is submitted. The detector reports how many milliseconds remain until a flush is required.
When idle, -1 is returned. No timeout is needed.
When waiting or absorbing, the remaining time is computed as the burst threshold minus the elapsed time.
If the remaining time is negative, 0 is returned to indicate that the timer has already expired.
If the remaining time is zero or positive, that value is returned as the number of milliseconds until the timeout fires.
When the elapsed time equals exactly 5 milliseconds, the remaining time is zero, and this operation returns 0 (indicating already expired). However, arrow-processing and timeout-checking both treat an elapsed time of exactly 5 milliseconds as within the burst window (their comparisons are inclusive). A caller that observes a return value of 0 here and immediately invokes timeout-checking with the same timestamp will receive none rather than a flushed event.
behavior.immediate-flush:
The detector is instructed to flush any pending buffered event immediately, regardless of the timer.
When waiting, the buffered pending arrow is emitted as a keyboard event: arrow-up if the pending direction is up, arrow-down if the pending direction is down. The detector transitions to idle.
When idle or absorbing, none is returned. The detector transitions to idle regardless of which of these two states it was in.
When the detector is in the absorbing state, flushing transitions it to idle and returns none, without any indication that an in-progress burst was interrupted. The scroll event was already emitted when the burst was first detected, so no additional event is needed; however, any remaining arrows in the burst that have not yet been absorbed will begin a new detection cycle.

state-transitions.transition: Waiting → Waiting (emits keyboard arrow for old pending; new arrow becomes pending)
Absorbing → Waiting (new arrow becomes pending; returns none)
